coredata.dc:title: “ESIPT-AIE” based sequential fluorescence ‘on-off’ marker for endogenous detection of hypochlorite and cobalt (II)
coredata.dc:creator: Naha, Sanay
Velmathi, Sivan
coredata.dc:description: Discrimination of a specific metal ion and ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) among a library of ionic species in a water medium is one of the daunting tasks and it has been a hot-bed in the aspect of analytical chemistry. In the same objective, we have designed a tailor-fit molecule which serves as a sequential detector for ROS (−OCl) and a transition metal ion (Co2+). The novel receptor R has been derived from a simple condensation reaction between 2,5-thiophenedicarboxaldehyde and p-toluenesulfonyl hydrazide. The receptor is efficient for hypochlorite detection among all anions and other reactive oxygen species via ‘turn-on’ emission in 20% water (HEPES buffer, pH = 8) + DMSO medium without any interference through oxidative chemodosimetric mechanism. And the resultant emissive solution is extremely efficient for Co2+ among all cations via both change in colour and ‘turn-off’ emission. The obtained results are well supported by theoretical calculations. Limit of detection for hypochlorite and Co2+ are found to be 0.53 nM and 0.97 µM, respectively. The receptor showed real-time working efficiency in a bleach solution and chocolate extract. The receptor is proven to be biocompatible and is applied for endogenous fluorescence cell imaging of hypochlorite and Co2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ESIPT-AIE
Hypochlorite
Relay recognition
Endogenous detection
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Emission response of receptor R with all the anions under UV light, (b) Naked eye colorimetric response of receptor R with anions under normal light. The receptor concentration maintained as 25 µM and anions concentrations are 50 µM in 20% Water (HEPES buffer, pH=8) + DMSO.
(a) Electronic spectrum of the receptor R with anions, (b) Emission spectrum of the receptor R with all the anions. The excitation wavelength is 354 nm.

(a) Naked eye response of receptor R with ROS, (b) Photograph of receptor R with all the ROS under UV light, (c) Electronic spectra of receptor R and other ROS and (d) Emission spectra of receptor R with all ROS, where hypochlorite shows selectivity among all ROS with ‘turn-on’ emission. The receptor concentration maintained as 25 µM and ROS concentration are 50 µM.

(a) Emission of receptor R with hypochlorite in various pH medium under UV light, (b) Naked eye colorimetric response of R with hypochlorite in various pH medium, (c) Comparative emission intensity plot of R and R +−OCl in various pH medium at 463 nm.

(a) Response of receptor R for relay recognition of Co2+ among all cations at pH = 8 under UV light, (b) Naked eye response of receptor R for relay recognition of Co2+ among all cations at pH = 8, (c) Emission spectra and (d) Electronic spectra of receptor for relay recognition of Co2+ among all cations where Co2+ efficiently quench the R+−OCl emission with an intense brown colouration.

(a) Interference study for hypochlorite selectivity in the presence of ROS. The comparative plot of emission intensity at the wavelength 463 nm between R+ROS and R+ROS+−OCl is showing the selectivity of hypochlorite in the presence of ROS (1= Only R, 2= Citrate, 3 = L-Cysteine, 4 = H2O2, 5= OONO−, 6= NO•, 7 = O2 −, 8= −OCl, 9= HO•, and 10= 1O2), (b) Interference study for Co2+ selectivity in presence of other metal ions. The comparative plot of emission intensity at the wavelength 463 nm between R+−OCl+Mn+ and R+−OCl+Mn++Co2+ is showing selective emission quenching by Co2+ even in the presence of other metal ions (1= Only R, 2 = R (pH 8), 3 = R+−OCl, 4= Al3+, 5= Cd2+, 6= Co2+, 7= Cr3+, 8= Cu2+, 9= Hg2+, 10= Ni2+ , 11= Pb2+, and 12= Zn2+).

Kinetics of hypochlorite sensing using emission spectroscopy showing maximum emission intensity, saturates after 25 min of two equivalent hypochlorite addition. The relay recognition of Co2+ by quenching of emission is very spontaneous.

(a) Incremental titration of R against −OCl maintaining a constant addition of 0.2 equivalent of −OCl with a 20 min time interval, (b) The correlation plot between emission intensity of R after addition of respective equivalence of −OCl (c) Incremental titration of R+−OCl (2 Eq.) against Co2+ marinating a constant addition of 0.1 equivalent of Co2+, (d) The correlation plot between emission intensity of R+ −OCl (2 Eq.) after addition of respective equivalence Co2+ and equivalence of Co2+.
Lifetime measurement of excited states of R, R+−OCl and R+−OCl+Co2+.
Proposed sensing mechanism of −OCl/Co2+ relay detection by receptor R.
The optimized molecular framework of (a) the only R and (b) R+ClO−; (c) The theoretical molecular energy diagram of only R, the ground state of R+ClO− and the excited state of R+ClO−.

(a) Electronic spectra of real sample analysis. The receptor R is applied against bleach solution (−OCl real sample) and chocolate extract (Co2+ real sample), (b) Emission response of receptor R for real sample analysis.
MTT assay experiment of receptor R on RAW264.7 cells.

Confocal fluorescence images of RAW264.7 cells. (a-d) Control experiment of RAW264.7, (e–h) the cells were incubated with R (20 μM) for 30 min., (i-l) The cells were further incubated with PMA (2 μg/mL) for 2 hrs. (Endogenous hypochlorite detection), (m-p). Finally the cells were treated with Co2+ (30 μM) and incubated for another 30 min. The images were taken at λex = 420 nm. Scale bars = 10 μm.

head.ce:title: “ESIPT-AIE” based sequential fluorescence ‘on-off’ marker for endogenous detection of hypochlorite and cobalt (II)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic and Polymer Synthesis Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology-Tiruchirappalli, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Organic and Polymer Synthesis Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology-Tiruchirappalli, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Discrimination of a specific metal ion and ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) among a library of ionic species in a water medium is one of the daunting tasks and it has been a hot-bed in the aspect of analytical chemistry. In the same objective, we have designed a tailor-fit molecule which serves as a sequential detector for ROS (−OCl) and a transition metal ion (Co2+). The novel receptor R has been derived from a simple condensation reaction between 2,5-thiophenedicarboxaldehyde and p-toluenesulfonyl hydrazide. The receptor is efficient for hypochlorite detection among all anions and other reactive oxygen species via ‘turn-on’ emission in 20% water (HEPES buffer, pH = 8) + DMSO medium without any interference through oxidative chemodosimetric mechanism. And the resultant emissive solution is extremely efficient for Co2+ among all cations via both change in colour and ‘turn-off’ emission. The obtained results are well supported by theoretical calculations. Limit of detection for hypochlorite and Co2+ are found to be 0.53 nM and 0.97 µM, respectively. The receptor showed real-time working efficiency in a bleach solution and chocolate extract. The receptor is proven to be biocompatible and is applied for endogenous fluorescence cell imaging of hypochlorite and Co2+.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemically reactive species are omnipresent in natural processes such as xenobiotic, infectious agents and exogenous exposure to UV-light, and endogenous mitochondrial catalytic respiration by a heme-enzyme Myeloperoxidase (MPO) [1–3]. Endogenous production of ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) is primarily driven by vital biological processes using molecular oxygen. The ROS which are majorly abundant are H2O2, OH•, ROO•, 1O2, O2 − and (HOCl/−OCl) [4–10]. Hypochlorite and its protonated form are of ample significant because of reaction vulnerability, fast reaction time, and comparatively long lifetime and essential for cellular processes [11–14]. Hypochlorite has been used in large scale for the sewage and cooling water treatment, hospital laundry sterilization, bleaching agent for drinking water and in textile industries, disinfecting, and cyanide treatment [15–19]. Though hypochlorite plays a vital role in biological processes such as signal transduction, destroying pathogens, anti-microbial activity, but, excess exposure to hypochlorite can lead to inflammation, carcinogenesis, neurodegenerative injury, neuron degeneration, rheumatoid arthritis, cardiovascular disease [20–25]. Hence, regulation on optimum tolerance limit of hypochlorite in both commercial and environmental systems is of huge urgency. Alongside anionic analytes, an overdose of cationic analytes is also endangered to a living organism. Though the presence of metal ions in a biological system is very minute, the impact of metal ions on the biological processes is ever-present. In major, among the transition metal ions, cobalt, in particular, has both valuable and injurious effects on human health. Cobalt is constructive because of vitamin B12, which is a mandatory element to maintain human health, anaemia treatment especially pregnant women, even for various animals, such as cattle and sheep [26,27]. In general, cobalt is not harmful to mankind but too much intake of cobalt is harmful to health and can cause breathing problem, affect lungs, asthma, wheezing, skin rashes, and pneumonia. The abnormal level of cobalt metabolism can result in some severe neurological diseases involving Alzheimer's disease, epilepsy, and Parkinson's disease [27]. So quantitative regulation of cobalt is also very important. Due to the significant roles of −OCl and Co2+ in various aspect of biomedical field, some tedious effort has been employed to develop numerous representative analytical techniques including ultraviolet/atomic absorption spectrometry [28], ion chromatography [29], chemiluminescence [30], and atmospheric pressure ionization/ inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (API/ICP-MS) [31–33]. In comparison to the institutional instruments, fluorescence-based optical detection is much more feasible and suitable for rapid and real-time application. The instrumental procedures are a time-consuming, cost-effective, complex operational procedure, laborious pre-treatment process, and moreover, not accessible and applicable for real-time application to the majority of the society [34–36]. So, designing of the fluorometric and colorimetric probe for nanomolar detection of hypochlorite and Co2+ is a vibrant aspect.

In recent time, extensive reports have been made for selective and specific detection of hypochlorite ions based on thiol [37], oxime [38–40], hydroxylamine [41], dibenzoyl hydrazide [42], methoxyphenol derivative [43-44], rhodamine and fluorescein derivative [45–48], BODIPY derivative [49], modified nano particles [50], quantum dots [51], acenaphthenequinone derivative [52], metal complexes [53,54], graphene based [55], metal-organic framework [56,57], coumarin based [58] and derivative of various fluorophores. Majorly, the sensing mechanism adopted for hypochlorite sensing is oxidative interconversion of functional groups [59,60]. Very few literatures are available on only Co2+ detection [61,62]. There are few reports available on relay recognition mechanism for hypochlorite and metal ions, but there is no report on relay recognition of hypochlorite and Co2+ in semi-aqueous medium till date based on our knowledge.

On the above-mentioned note, the first time we propose a judicious design of a novel fluorometric ‘off-on-off’ dual smart sensor for hypochlorite and cobalt in the semi-aqueous medium through relay recognition mechanism. The formation of the receptor R is confirmed using IR, 1H & 13C NMR and HR-mass spectroscopic techniques, which is finally confirmed by elemental analysis. The emission after hypochlorite is due to ‘inhibition of ESIPT and simultaneously ‘turn-on’ AIE process. The experimental observations are rightly supported by theoretical calculations. The detailed results are demonstrated in the results and discussion section.

Analytical grade 2,5-thiophenedicarboxaldehyde, p-Toluenesulfonyl Hydrazide, all metals in the form of chloride salts and tetrabutylammonium salts of all anions were obtained from Sigma Aldrich and used. Sodium hypochlorite was from MERCK and used without any further purification. Spectroscopic grade solvents were used for spectroscopic experiments. Absorbance spectra were obtained in Shimadzu UV-2600; Luminescence spectra were recorded on Shimadzu RF-5301 pc spectrophotometer; High resolution multinuclear FT-NMR spectrometer AV- 500-Bruker 500 MHz is used for 1H & 13C measurements. Elementar Vario EL III used for elemental analysis maintaining digestion temperature at 950 – 1200 °C. Horiba Delta time instrument coupled with TCSPC used for luminescence decay rate measurement studies. Multiskan GO microplate reader used for MTT assay absorbance measurement. The Leica TCS SP5 X AOBS Confocal Fluorescence Microscope (Germany) with a 63x oil-immersion objective lens in cyan channel used for emission imaging of cells.

2,5-Thiophenedicarboxaldehyde (140 mg, 1 mM) was dissolved using 5 mL of ethanol in a 250 mL round bottom flask. After getting a transparent yellow solution, a catalytic amount of trifluoroacetic acid (22 mg, 0.2 mM, 15 µM) was added and stirred the solution for a further 10 min. Then p-Toluenesulfonyl hydrazide (372 mg, 2 mM) was added to the resultant solution and reflux for 2 hrs. The progress of the reaction was monitored by TLC. After 2 hrs of reflux, the reaction mixture was allowed to get cool to room temperature and a bright pink colour precipitate appeared. The precipitate was separated using filtration, followed by the precipitate was washed using hot ethanol and kept for drying. Yield: 426 mg (90%) (Scheme S1).
Melting point: 210–212 ᵒC.

IR (KBr, ν -cm
−
1
): 3508 (Adsorbed Water), 3165 (S=0 str.), 2972 (S-N str.), 2922 (N-N str.), 2859, (N-H str.), 1595 (C=N str.), 1442 (C-N str.), 710 (N-H bending) (Fig. S1).
LCMS (HRMS) m/z : [M+H] + Calcd. for C20H20N4O4S3, 476.59, Found: 475.06 [M-H] (Fig. S5).

The receptor R is designed from 2,5-thiophenedicarboxaldehyde and p-toluene sulfonyl hydrazine. Sulfonyl group is known to possess the aggregation-induced luminescence property and 2,5-thiphenedicarboxaldehyde acts as dipodal symmetric linker. The [-NH] group of the hydrazone moiety is susceptible for deprotonation or, oxidation by basic analytes or, ROS respectively. Based on the assumption, the receptor R was employed for selectivity experiment for both anions and ROS. Interestingly, the receptor R is detecting selectively hypochlorite by “turn-on” emission response followed by detection of Co2+ by “turn-off” response, but no anions have any significant impact on the receptor R. The relay recognition of hypochlorite and Co2+ along with other competing ions were studied using electronic, emission, and 1H NMR spectroscopic techniques. The interference of other competing analytes in sensing and kinetics of sensing are also examined. The real-time applicability and biocompatibility of the receptor was investigated and successfully applied for endogenous cell imaging of hypochlorite and Co2+. The obtained results for each consecutive experiments are documented as follows:

Solvent plays a pivotal role in chemical reactivity, molecular association, rate of a reaction, solubility and stability of a material. So the choice for the appropriate working medium is of obvious importance. To find a solvent effect on the receptor, a range of solvents have been chosen from non-polar to high-polar. It has been observed that polar solvents provide a suitable and optimum platform for maximum working efficiency. Among the polar solvents, DMSO is observed to be the best solvent as high polar, miscibility with water, and biocompatible are the prime advantages (For Figure see ESI Fig. S6 and S7).

The receptor R was applied for cation sensing in DMSO medium and there was no significant indication of receptor and cations interaction. Then the receptor was applied for naked eye anion sensing. Firstly, 25 µM solution of the receptor were prepared using DMSO and 2 equivalent of a library of anions such as CN−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, AcO−, HSO4 −, H2PO4 −, NO3 −, OH−, PF6 − and −OCl were added to the vials. There was no appreciable colorimetric and fluorometric response. As soon as, the sensing medium was changed to 20% water (HEPES buffer, pH=8) + DMSO, a ‘turn-on’ emission was observed only for hypochlorite among the library of anions (Fig. 1 ). Hence, the receptor is showing selectivity and specificity for hypochlorite in 20% aqueous DMSO (pH=8) solvent and the same solvent combination is maintained throughout all photo-physical studies. The emission is attributed to aggregation-induced emission (AIE).

The electronic and emission spectrum of receptor R with all anions were recorded in 20% water (HEPES) + DMSO medium (Fig. 2 a and 2b). The absorbance spectrum of receptor R consists of three major peaks which are at 264 nm, 354 nm, and 420 nm. Among all the peaks, peak at 354 nm is attributed as π-π* electronic transition with the highest intensity whereas the peak at 420 nm is signifying n-π* transition which is significantly less in intensity and the peak at 264 nm is for the n-σ* transition. On addition of Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, CN−, PF6 −, absorbance spectrum did not change, while other anions did show an increase in absorbance intensity of 420 nm peak and simultaneously decrease in absorbance intensity of 354 nm peak. But in the case of the electronic spectrum of R with ClO−, the peak at 354 nm disappeared completely and spectrum showed an increase in absorbance intensity to 0.6 from 0.2 along with red-shift in absorbance maxima from 354 nm to 420 nm followed by the appearance of a new peak at around 300 nm. However, in the emission spectrum, the only hypochlorite is selectively recognized by receptor R via ‘turn-on’ emission with λem = 463 nm.

Hypochlorite is one of the long-lived reactive oxygen species (ROS) among ROS family. Since receptor shown selectivity for hypochlorite, selectivity experiment for ROS is also important to prove its specificity only for hypochlorite. The ROS solutions were prepared using the standard method and used freshly prepared. To 25 µM in 3 mL solution of the receptor solutions, 100 µL of 1.5 × 10−3 M ROS were introduced. From naked-eye observation, ROS did show any effect except intensification of yellowish color (Fig. 3 a). Under UV-light, it is confirmed that among all ROS, only −OCl is capable to ‘turn-on’ cyan emission in the receptor which can be detectable even in the naked eye (Fig. 3b). Electronic and emission spectrum are supportive of the selectivity and specificity for hypochlorite among all ROS (Fig. 3c and 3d).

pH of a working solution has a profound effect on the stability, reactivity, physical property and bio-applicability of a system. In the environment, mostly systems are working in basic medium and ROS detection in real-time also involve basic medium. So pH effect on receptor's hypochlorite detection efficiency is an accountable aspect. HEPES buffer has been used to prepare and maintain the pH of the working solution. Free receptor did not show any observable inference in any of the respective pH solutions. But, upon introduction of −OCl to each of the pH solutions a naked eye observable yellow colour appears for pH solutions 7–12 (Fig. 4 a). Under UV-light, pH solution 6–12 are capable to show emission whereas pH below 6 is not showing any emission (Fig. 4b). The naked eye and under UV-light observations are aptly supported by electronic and emission responses (Fig. S8a and S8b). The comparison plot of emission response of only R and R+ClO− at 463 is evident for the working efficiency in various basic pH (Fig. 4c). So, receptor can work in basic medium to recognize hypochlorite.

Recognition capability of the receptor was further examined for relay recognition of metal ions to obtain a sequential detector for hypochlorite and metal ion. Firstly, 3 mL of 25 µM receptor solutions were prepared in 20% aqueous DMSO. Followed by, to each of the vials 50 µM −OCl were added. After the addition of hypochlorite, 2 equivalent of respective cations were added and responses were recorded. The naked eye image is self-explanatory of Co2+ recognition by R+−OCl product via a change in color to brownish (Fig. 5 a). Along with color change, upon Co2+ addition the emission of the solution gets quenched by 6 folds which is sufficiently accountable in the emission spectra (Fig. 5b and 5c). The UV–Vis spectra of the relay recognition showed the increase in absorbance intensity without changing the peak positions at 420 nm and 300 nm, followed by the appearance of a hump around 500 nm, which is the evidence of the Co2+ complexation with the oxidised R (Fig. 5d).

The extent of perturbation by the competing counter analytes to the sensing process is one of the aspiring aspects to look at. In the presence of other analytes, if the receptor is capable to recognize the target analytes with optimum efficiency, it would be a great positive sign for its real-time applicability and precision in detection. The interference experiment examined for both −OCl/Co2+ recognition. For −OCl experiment, all other ROSs were used as competing ions for the analyte, while for Co2+, all cations were used. The receptor did not get agitated by other ROS and as a result even in the presence of other ROS, the receptor can detect preciously −OCl with ‘turn-on’ emission (Fig. 6 a). In the case of Co2+ recognition, R+−OCl showed its efficiency to detect selectively and specifically Co2+ even in the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 6b). So receptor is selective and specific for −OCl/Co2+ pair.

The receptor is found to be an efficient one for −OCl/Co2+ through relay recognition process via ‘off-on-off’ emission response. The intensity of emission upon addition of −OCl is perceived as time-dependent phenomena. So the time required to show the optimum intensity and complete detection process is in need. To obtain the time period for completion of the reaction, the kinetic study has been examined. The receptor showed the maximum intensity of emission after a time period of 25 min, and thereafter intensity remains intact (Fig. 7 ). On addition of two equivalent Co2+in the resultant solution, the emission gets quenched immediately which is evident from the plot.

Quantification of the target analyte by the receptor is the utmost parameter to evaluate the quality of receptor utility. In the incremental titration of receptor R by hypochlorite, a gradual increase in emission intensity has been observed at 463 nm with the increase of hypochlorite concentration and receptor R showed saturation maxima at 2 equivalent of hypochlorite concentration (Fig. 8 a and 8b). Similarly, incremental titration of R+−OCl by Co2+ showed a gradual decrease in emission intensity and showed saturation minima at a concentration of 1 equivalent of Co2+ (Fig. 8c and 8d). Job's plot for hypochlorite and Co2+ detection showed stoichiometry 1:2 and 1:1 respectively, which are in correlation with saturation concentrations (Fig. S9a and S9b). Optimum limit of detection of hypochlorite and Co2+ are 0.53 nM and 0.97 µM respectively (Fig. S10a and S10b). The binding constant for Co2+ recognition by R+−OCl is 1.4 × 104 M−1 (Fig. S11). The photochemical yields (ɸ) for only R, R+−OCl and R+−OCl+Co2+ are 0.17, 0.57 and 0.2 respectively where Rhodamine B is taken as reference (ɸref = 0.95) (Table S1).

The emission lifetime decay experiment of the receptor was examined using 455 nano-LED source since the emission wavelength of R+ClO− is at λem= 463 nm (Fig. 9 ). The average luminescence lifetime of R, R+−OCl and R+−OCl+Co2+ were found to be 6.2 ps, 13 ps and 4.8 ps respectively. The triple-exponential decay theoretical equation was used to validate the experimental luminescence decay curve of R, R+−OCl and R+−OCl+Co2+. It is evident that there is a definite increase in luminescence lifetime of the oxidised R by 6.8 ps when hypochlorite ion oxidized [NH-] functional group to [-N-OH] of R, and hence, the excited state of oxidised R is more stable than that of the only R. On the other hand, the resultant decrease in average lifetime of R+−OCl+ Co2+ is 8.2 ps, which is the evidence for the unstable excited state and ultimately renders to quenching of emission.

The 1H NMR sequential titration was carried out to examine the hypochlorite detection by R was proved by titration. R (0.01 M) and NaOCl (0.01 M) were prepared in DMSO-d6 for titrations, where 0.0–2.0 Eq. of hypochlorite was added sequentially and 1H NMR spectrum were presented in Fig. 10 . As far as the molecular framework of the receptor is concerned, among all present functional groups only amide [-NH] can be further oxidized. On introduction of 2 equivalent hypochlorite to R, the peak at 11.56 ppm disappeared completely which is corresponding to amide [–NH]. The professed accumulation of the charge around the sulfonyl hydrazide functionality on −OCl addition has noticeable input on its neighbor proton signals in the 1H NMR spectra.

From 1H NMR studies and incremental titration, it is comprehended that oxidation of amide [-NH] by hypochlorite is the leading driving force for the selective and specific recognition of hypochlorite over other ROS without any interference. The oxidation of amide [-NH] comprises of two intermediate steps, which are chlorination of amide [-NH] by −OCl, followed by hydrolysis by a water molecule (Fig. 11 ), which is supported by HR-MS (Fig. S12). From the titrimetric plot, it is obvious that 2 equivalent addition of hypochlorite is sufficient to saturate the receptor, since the receptor R consists of two symmetric amide [-NH] functionality. Followed by, detection of Co2+ is governing through complexation between oxidized R and Co2+, which is supported by IR spectra of the complex (Fig. S13). The entire sequential recognition of ClO−/Co2+ by “off-on-off” fluorometric response is governed by “ESIPT off-AIE on-AIE off” [63-65]. The receptor R does not possess any emission, which is due to ESIPT (Excited State Intramolecular Proton Transfer) mechanism between proton of [-NH] and the oxygen atom of [-S=O] (Fig. S14). Enhancement of fluorescence by oxidized R is governed by inhibition of ESIPT mechanism and turning on AIE (Aggregation Induced Emission) mechanism, as sulfonyl moiety is known as an AIE favourable luminophore (Fig. S15). Upon complexation with Co2+, quenching of fluorescence took place which is attributed as turn off AIE process.

From the AIE experiment, it was seen that the hypochlorite treated receptor showed an increase in green emission on increasing water content at 463 nm in the emission spectrum, but there was no significant color change took place. To support these experimental observations, theoretical density functional calculations (DFT) were performed to determine the energy of molecular orbitals of R and R+ClO− in water.

Elucidation of conformational optimization and expected energy states were performed using Gaussian 09 software and Gauss View 5 GUI was used to envisage obtained results. Geometry optimizations were conducted using (B3LYP) functional with basic set 6-31 G/dp and the water solvation factor were incorporated (Fig. 12 a and b). The energy of the excited state is calculated using TDDFT/B3LYP function. HOMO and LUMO of R and R+ClO− were engendered from the optimized structures (Fig. 12c).

From the calculated results, the energy requirement between the HOMO and LUMO of ground state R+ClO− had decreased in comparison to R, which is further decreased for the excited state of R+ClO−. The calculated expected energy gap for R, ground state R+ClO− and excited state R+ClO− are 3.38 eV (λ=366 nm), 2.78 eV (λ=446 nm) and 1.9 eV (λ=649 nm). As the AIE is a ground state phenomenon, the calculated energy gap between HOMO and LUMO of R+ClO− in the ground state is 446 nm which is well coincided with the observed absorbance wavelength 463 (±17 nm) nm.

The receptor showed its efficiency to work as a sequential detector for −OCl/Co2+ in 20% aqueous medium which does not get hampered due to the pH (>7) of the working solution. As receptor is able to work in slightly alkaline medium with optimum efficiency, the receptor's applicability has been extended for real sample analysis, cytotoxicity and endogenous fluorescence bio-imaging. The results obtained are as follows:

To prove the receptor's applicability in real-time, commercially available bleach solution taken as hypochlorite source and extract of chocolate taken as Co2+ source. Bleach solution is used for disinfecting surface during water treatment, while chocolate is proven to be the highest content of cobalt metal among the major eatable items. So commercially available bleaching solution and chocolate are used for real sample analysis. Firstly, 300 g of commercially available chocolate (Dairy Milk) was taken in a 250 mL conical flack and treated with 50 mL of distilled hexane to remove the fatty acids and butter content. Then 50 mL of ethyl acetate was added and the organic part was extracted using 100 mL of double-distilled water. The collected water part is used as a real sample for Co2+ source. To the 25 µM of 3 mL receptor's solution, 100 µL of commercially available bleach solution introduced which resulted in 70 nm red shift of maxima wavelength which is exactly correlating with the results obtained in the case of only −OCl, but absorbance is lesser than the only −OCl. To the same solution, upon addition of 100 µL of chocolate extract, the absorbance maxima again blue-shifted to 354 nm from 420 nm. Emission response obtained on addition of real sample is as expected. On addition of bleach solution, enhancement in emission observed, which is quenched by the addition of chocolate extract. So the receptor is able to detect −OCl/Co2+ in the real sample also (Fig. 13 ).

The biocompatibility of a receptor is of ample importance in terms of bio-applicability and imaging purpose. So to check its biocompatibility within the working concentration, MTT assay using RAW264.7 cell line is examined. The methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay was employed to evaluated toxicity parameter of R in RAW264.7 cell line (Fig. 14 ). 96-well cell-culture plate was used to grow RAW264.7 cells and various concentrations of R were added ranging from 5 to 25 µM to the wells and incubated at 37 ᵒC under 5% CO2 for 24 h. Followed by, wells were incubated with 10 µL MTT (5 mg mL−1) at 37 ᵒC under 5% CO2 for 4 h. Upon washing the MTT dye, 200 mL DMSO and 25 mL sorensen's glycine buffer (0.1 M glycine and 0.1 M NaCl) solution is used for dissolving the yellow precipitate (formazan). The absorbance for each well was examined at 570 nm. The viability of the cells were calculated by the following equation:
Cell viability (%) = (Mean of absorbance value of treatment group)/ (Mean of absorbance value of control group)

Since MTT assay experimental results are satisfactory and the toxicity of receptor R is within tolerable limit, the receptor R is employed for fluorescence cell imaging of −OCl/Co2+ pair employed in RAW264.7 cell line [66]. RAW264.7 cells were grown in DMEM media with 10% FBS and penicillin/streptomycin maintaining 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubation condition. After treating the grown cells with R (20 µM, 2% DMSO), cells were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Followed by, an intense cyan fluorescence in cells was observed on the addition of PMA (2 µg/mL) to the R-treated cells. On sequential addition of Co2+ (30 µM) in the culture medium, the cyan emission in RAW264.7 cells disappeared. The cell images were obtained by exciting the cells below 420 nm using a UV-Lamp and luminescence was recorded at 463 nm ± 30 nm (Fig. 15 ).

Ratiometric, sequential, fluorogenic receptor R has been developed with ‘off-on-off’ emission response for −OCl/Co2+ detection in semi-aqueous medium with no significant influence from competent analytes. The sensing mechanism of hypochlorite is ascertained as an oxidative-chemodosimetric approach, as hypochlorite oxidized [-NH] functionality to [-N-OH], which is the reason of efficient ceasing of the ESIPT process leading to ‘turn-on’ AIE emission. On the contrary, binding of Co2+ with the oxidized-R facilitated LMCT mechanism leading to ‘turn-off’ AIE emission. The limit of detection for −OCl and Co2+ are 0.53 nM and 0.97 µM respectively. The oxidized-R showed appreciable binding affinity specifically for Co2+ with a binding constant of 1.4 × 104 M−1. The receptor R has performed exceedingly well in real sample analysis and in endogenous fluorescence confocal imaging of RAW264.7 cell. Bioactivity of the receptor R can be further studied for important biological activity such as anti-fungal, anti-bacterial, anti-inflammatory or as an anti-cancer drug.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors do not have any conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Author SN acknowledges DST-INSPIRE Fellowship (IF150881) scheme for supporting financially. Authors thank Prof. Shu Pao Wu of National Chiao Tung University, Taiwan for helping with live-cell bio imaging studies. Authors thank STIC (Sophisticated Test and Instrumentation centre), Kochi University for helping in elemental analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2019.104499.
